g.data-narration: Welcome to C Programming Fundamentals. Learn the building blocks of one of the world's most influential programming languages.
C was developed by Dennis Ritchie at Bell Labs in 1972. It forms the foundation for languages like C++, Java, Python, and C#.
g.text: Developed by Dennis Ritchie at Bell Laboratories in 1972
Foundation for C++, Java, Python, and C#. Essential for system software,

g.data-narration: Learning C develops computational thinking, problem-solving skills, and algorithmic approaches that transfer to any programming paradigm.
g.text: Essential for GATE exams and core IT company placements

g.data-narration: Every C program has a specific structure: preprocessor directives, main function as entry point, declarations, and executable statements.
C provides primary data types: int for integers, float for single precision, double for double precision, and char for characters.
Variables are named memory locations storing values that change during execution. They must follow naming rules: begin with letter/underscore, case-sensitive, no keywords.
Constants are fixed values that never change. Use #define for macros or const keyword for read-only variables.
Arithmetic operators perform mathematical calculations: addition (+), subtraction (-), multiplication (*), division (/), and modulus (%).
Relational operators compare values (==, !=, >, <, >=, <=). Logical operators combine conditions (&&, ||, !).

g.text: Example: (a > 5) && (b < 10) - both conditions must be true

g.data-narration: Increment (++) adds 1, decrement (--) subtracts 1. Prefix increments before use; postfix increments after returning original value.
Assignment operators combine arithmetic with assignment: +=, -=, *=, /=, %= provide shorthand for operations like x = x + 5 becoming x += 5.
printf() outputs data using format specifiers: %d for int, %f for float, %c for char, %s for string. Use %.2f for 2 decimal places.
scanf() reads input using format specifiers. IMPORTANT: Always use & (address-of operator) for variables, except for character arrays.
Type conversion happens implicitly or explicitly. Integer division truncates decimals. Use 3.0 or explicit cast (float) for floating-point results.

g.text: average = (a + b) / 3.0; // 3.0 makes it float division

g.data-narration: Operator precedence hierarchy: Parentheses first, then unary, multiplicative, additive, relational, logical, assignment. Use parentheses to clarify!
Remember these key exam tips: use correct format specifiers, use & in scanf, operator precedence matters, integer vs float division, and sizeof() returns bytes.
